analogy: Think of "Social Institutions Continuity and Change" through the chapter's own opening images: Social Institutions: Continuity and Change 35 Indian Society 36 Having studied the structure and dynamics of the population of India in Chapter 2, we turn now to the study of social institutions. A population is not just a collection of separate, unrelated individuals, it is... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: 3.1 C aste and the Caste system, 3.3 Family and Kinship, NUCLEAR AND EXTENDED FAMILY, Social Institutions Continuity and Change. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Social Institutions Continuity and Change is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that social Institutions: Continuity and Change 35

The text explains that indian Society 36 Having studied the structure and dynamics of the population of India in Chapter 2, we turn now to the study of social institutions.

The text explains that a population is not just a collection of separate, unrelated individuals, it is a society made up of distinct but interlinked classes and communities of various kinds.

The text explains that these communities are sustained and regulated by social institutions and social relationships.

The text explains that in this chapter we will be looking at three institutions that are central to Indian society, namely caste, tribe and family.

The text explains that 3.1 C aste and the Caste system Like any Indian, you already know that ‘caste’ is the name of an ancient social institution that has been part of Indian history and culture for thousands of years.

The text explains that but like any Indian living in the twenty-first century, you also know that something called ‘caste’ is definitely a part of Indian society today.

The text explains that to what extent are these two ‘castes’ – the one that is supposed to be part of India’s past, and the one that is part of its present – the same thing?

The text explains that this is the question that we will try to answer in this section.

The text explains that caste in the Past Caste is an institution uniquely associated with the Indian sub-continent.

The text explains that while social arrangements producing similar effects have existed in other parts of the world, the exact form has not been found elsewhere.

The text explains that although it is an institution characteristic of Hindu society, caste has spread to the major non-Hindu communities of the Indian sub-continent.

The text explains that this is specially true of Muslims, Christians and Sikhs.

The text explains that as is well-known, the English word ‘caste’ is actually a borrowing from the Portuguese casta, meaning pure breed.

The text explains that the word refers to a broad institutional arrangement that in Indian languages (beginning with the ancient Sanskrit) is referred to by two distinct terms, varna and jati.

The text explains that varna, literally ‘colour’, is the name given to a four-fold division of society into brahmana, kshatriya, vaishya and shudra, though this excludes a significant section of the population composed of the ‘outcastes’, foreigners, slaves, conquered peoples and others, sometimes refered to as the panchamas or fifth category.

The text explains that the most common interpretation is to treat varna as a broad all-India aggregative classification, while jati is taken to be a regional or local sub-classification involving a much more complex system

The text explains that opinions also differ on the exact age of the caste system.

The text explains that however, the ‘caste system’ stood for different things in different time periods, so that it is misleading to think of the same system continuing for three thousand years.

The text explains that in its earliest phase, in the late Vedic period roughly between 900 — 500 BC, the caste system was really a varna system and consisted of only four major divisions.

The text explains that the most commonly cited defining features of caste are the following: 1.

The text explains that caste involves a system consisting of many castes arranged in a hierarchy of rank and status.

The text explains that indian Society 38 On the other hand, a particular occupation could only be pursued by the caste associated with it – members of other castes could not enter the occupation.

The text explains that these features are the prescribed rules found in ancient scriptural texts.

The text explains that it is also clear from the historical evidence that caste was a very unequal institution – some castes benefitted greatly from the system, while others were condemned to a life of endless labour and subordination.

The text explains that theoretically, the caste system can be understood as the combination of two sets of principles, one based on difference and separation and the other on wholism and hierarchy.

The text explains that on the other hand, these different and separated castes do not have an individual existence – they can only exist in relation to a larger whole, the totality of society consisting of all castes.

The text explains that further, this societal whole or system is a hierarchical rather than egalitarian system.

The text explains that in other words, J o t i r a o G o v i n d r a o P h u l e denounced the injustice of the caste system and scorned its rules of purity and pollution.

The text explains that i n 1 8 7 3 h e f o u n d e d t h e Satyashodhak Samaj (Truth Seekers Society), which was devoted to securing human rights and social justice for low-caste people.

The text explains that social Institutions: Continuity and Change 39 each caste has its own place in the system which cannot be taken by any other caste.

The text explains that since caste is also linked with occupation, the system functions as the social division of labour, except that, in principle, it allows no mobility.

The text explains that colonialism and Caste Compared to the ancient past, we know a lot more about caste in our recent history.

The text explains that if modern history is taken to begin with the nineteenth century, then Indian Independence in 1947 offers a natural dividing line between the colonial period (roughly 150 years from around 1800 to 1947) and the post-Independence or post-colonial period (the seven decades from 1947 to the present day).

The text explains that first begun in the 1860s, the census became a regular ten-yearly exercise conducted by the British Indian government from 1881 onwards.

The text explains that the 1901 Census under the direction of Herbert Risley was particularly important as it sought to collect information on the social hierarchy of caste – i.e., the social order of precedence in particular regions, as to the position of each caste in the rank order.

The text explains that overall, scholars feel that this kind of direct attempt to count caste and to officially record caste status changed the institution itself.

The text explains that indian Society 40 The administration also took an interest in the welfare of downtrodden castes, referred to as the ‘depressed classes’ at that time.

The text explains that it was as part of these efforts that the Government of India Act of 1935 was passed which gave legal recognition to the lists or ‘schedules’ of castes and tribes marked out for special treatment by the state.

The text explains that (You will read more on untouchability and the struggles against it in Chapter 5 on social exclusion.) Thus colonialism brought about major changes in the institution of caste.

The text explains that he aroused people to realise that all men are equal, and that it is the birthright of every individual to enjoy liberty and equality.

The text explains that social Institutions: Continuity and Change 41 caste and explicitly wrote this into the Constitution.

The text explains that for example, appointments to government jobs took no account of caste, thus leaving the well-educated upper castes and the ill-educated or often illiterate lower castes to compete on “equal” terms.

The text explains that the development activity of the state and the growth of private industry also affected caste indirectly through the speeding up and intensification of economic change.

The text explains that in what ways can changes in social structure lead to changes in the family structure?

The text explains that explain the difference between matriliny and matriarchy.

The text explains that social Institutions: Continuity and Change 51 REFERENCES Deshpande, Satish.

The text explains that social Inequality in India: Profites of Caste, Class and Social Mobility.

The text explains that 1991. ‘The reproduction of inequality: Occupation, caste and family’, in Contributions to Indian Sociology.

The text explains that homo Hierarchicus: The Caste System and its Implications.

The text explains that john, Mary E., Jha, Pravin Kumar. and Jodhka, Surinder S. ed.

The text explains that contested Transformations: Changing Economies and Identities in Contemporary India.

Source: NCERT Class 12 Indian Society, Chapter 3: Social Institutions Continuity and Change
